quote: Hey there, Norm. This is Tanner with Ion Solar. How are you doing today? I'll be super quick here. I'm just reaching out to you because you filled out that form online about solar options for your home.
stewart_read: Tanner's intro is clean — company name stated, form reference included, human pause invited. This is the one structural beat Tanner executes correctly. It is worth noting because the rest of the call does not build on this foundation.
coaching_implication: Use this as the positive anchor in the 1-on-1. The intro is not the problem. The manager can affirm this beat and then focus the coaching conversation on what happened after it.

quote: I don't see there's a financial benefit to making the investment. We already have 11 panels.
stewart_read: This is the call's hinge moment. Norm delivers a reasoned ROI objection — not an emotional one — and Tanner bypasses it entirely to ask about the bill. Strategic read: Tanner may have been gathering data before reframing. Reactive read: Tanner had no reframe ready and defaulted to the next script step. The reactive read is supported by the fact that the bill figure captured at 00:53 was never used as leverage — if Tanner were setting up a flip, the bill number would have been the instrument. It was not.
alternative_hypothesis: Tanner was deliberately deferring the reframe until he had the bill figure in hand — a setup for a later flip.
coaching_implication: Show Tanner the objection_inversion framework. The bill figure ($100–$150 with a pool and spa) was the exact instrument for a flip: 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' The manager should walk through what that line sounds like and why it interrupts Norm's autopilot. No fabricated numbers needed — Norm's own figure is the leverage.

quote: Okay. But that's because we got a swimming pool and a spa.
stewart_read: Norm volunteers the pool and spa detail as a defensive explanation — he is neutralizing the bill figure before Tanner can use it, essentially saying 'the bill is high but it's not a solar problem, it's a load problem.' This is not an enthusiasm signal in the buying-signal sense; it is a customer pre-empting the pitch. However, the residual load is real: a pool, a spa, and 11 existing panels still producing a $100–$150 monthly bill is a meaningful anchor. Tanner does not use it.
alternative_hypothesis: The pool/spa detail is a buying signal indicating high load and therefore high solar value.
coaching_implication: The pool/spa context makes the residual bill more compelling, not less. Coach Tanner to reflect the detail back: 'So even with 11 panels, the pool and spa are still running up a $100–$150 bill — that's exactly the kind of load a supplemental design could address.' The customer handed Tanner the anchor; Tanner did not pick it up.

quote: No. I when I when I got online, I was just looking to see if New Mexico still gave, tax reduction form like we did last year.
stewart_read: Norm reveals that the form fill was not a solar purchase inquiry — it was a tax credit search. This reframes the entire lead: Norm was not shopping for solar, he was looking for a government incentive and landed on Ion's form. Tanner's response ('those have been expired since, like, I believe, the first of the year') is a knowledge-gap moment — the rep is confirming a policy detail with hedged language ('I believe') rather than redirecting to the specialist. More importantly, Tanner does not use this revelation to reframe the appointment as the place to get accurate, current incentive information.
coaching_implication: The tax credit context is a reframe opportunity: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist tracks — incentive windows, what's still available in New Mexico, what's changed. That's the conversation worth having.' Tanner instead confirms the bad news and moves on. Manager should show Tanner how the specialist-bridge converts a dead-end answer into a reason to book.

quote: No. I when I when I got online, I was just looking to see if New Mexico still gave, tax reduction form like we did last year.
stewart_read: At 01:00, Tanner correctly initiates the orphaned_flow by asking whether the existing panels were Ion panels — the first diagnostic step for a customer with existing solar. Norm answers 'no' and then immediately pivots to the tax credit context. Tanner drops the orphaned_flow entirely: no question about ownership type (own vs lease), no question about system status (working or not), no question about panel size. The codex requires these steps because they determine whether the customer is a DQ, a cross-sell, or a supplemental-design candidate. Tanner never finds out.
alternative_hypothesis: Tanner read Norm's pivot to the tax credit context as a signal that the orphaned_flow was irrelevant and chose to follow the customer's lead.
coaching_implication: Walk Tanner through the four orphaned_flow questions and explain why each matters. In this call specifically: if Norm owns his system and it is working, he is a supplemental-design candidate — the residual $100–$150 bill with a pool and spa is the hook. Tanner never established this. The manager should replay the 01:00–01:25 segment and ask: 'What did we not know at the end of this call that we should have known?'

quote: Yeah. No. Unfortunately, those have been expired since, like, I believe, the first of the year.
stewart_read: Tanner confirms the tax credit expiration with hedged language ('I believe') and delivers it as a dead-end rather than a bridge. The setter's scope discipline principle is clear: technical and policy questions belong to the specialist. Tanner is doing the specialist's job badly — confirming a policy detail with uncertainty — instead of routing the question to the closer where it can be answered accurately and used as a reason to book.
coaching_implication: This is a scope-creep moment that also happens to be a missed booking hook. The manager should use this line to illustrate the universal escape hatch from the codex: 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer — what's still available in New Mexico, what's changed this year. Let me get you on the calendar.' Tanner's hedged confirmation closes the door; the specialist-bridge opens it.

quote: the return on investment, you know, to get another five or 10 panels may drive the cost down to zero, but it would take me twenty years to pay for them. So, you know, financially, it doesn't make sense to me.
stewart_read: Norm's second and final ROI statement is more specific than the first — he has done the math and arrived at a twenty-year payback estimate. This is a reasoned objection, not a changed-mind or emotional resistance. The flip opportunity is still present: the bill figure ($100–$150) was captured at 00:53 and never used. A curiosity-creation move — 'Have you seen what a supplemental design actually looks like for your specific load, with the pool and spa factored in?' — does not require fabricated numbers and does not argue with his math. Tanner instead accepts the framing and exits.
alternative_hypothesis: Tanner correctly read Norm as a hard DQ — existing solar, no financial interest, tax credit expired — and chose not to waste further time.
coaching_implication: The alternative hypothesis is the reactive read dressed up as strategy. Norm is not a DQ: he is a homeowner with existing solar, a residual bill, and a high-load property. The twenty-year payback estimate is based on Norm's own math, not a specialist's design. The manager should ask Tanner: 'Did we ever show him a design? Did we ever ask if he'd seen what a supplemental system actually costs for his specific usage?' The answer is no — and that is the coaching gap.

quote: Okay. No. I hear you. Well, I mean, if anything changes, if you if you're ever open to looking into an estimate, I mean, you know where to call.
stewart_read: Tanner's exit line is the clearest evidence of single-angle handling. The codex target is 3+ angles per objection. Tanner attempted zero reframes across two touches of the same ROI objection. The soft exit ('you know where to call') has no follow-up mechanism — no locked callback, no text, no specialist referral. This is not a strategic retreat; it is a disengagement with no path back.
coaching_implication: Use this line as the coaching anchor for multi-angle resilience. The manager should ask: 'What was angle one? What was angle two? What was angle three?' The answer is that there were none. Then work backward: what were the three angles available in this call? (1) Flip the bill figure. (2) Bridge the tax credit question to the specialist. (3) Ask if Norm has seen a supplemental design for his specific load. None were deployed.
